artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Outcome-linked recognition has limited marginal effect; technical usefulness and local culture dominate behavior, so tying rewards to workflow outcomes mostly raises reporting overhead without meaningfully changing the mix of high-cost vs. low-cost workflows used.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In environments with very noisy or lagging outcome metrics, attempts to tie recognition to workflow-level outcomes backfire: teams cannot see a stable link between workflows and rewards, so they either ignore agents or overuse expensive workflows to signal effort, eroding trust and budget discipline.

question.text: In pay-as-you-go teams that already use workflow portfolios and exploration/production budget splits, what happens if leaders **tie a fixed share of each portfolio’s exploration budget to explicit “failure-tolerant quests”** (short, time-boxed missions to design or harden one repeatable workflow) instead of leaving exploration spend fully ad hoc—does this make pilot-to-scale adoption more repeatable by converting exploratory runs into promotable workflow families, or does it over-structure experimentation and reduce organic discovery of new agent uses?

artifacts.answer:
Tying a fixed share of each portfolio’s exploration budget to explicit, failure-tolerant quests usually makes pilot-to-scale adoption **more repeatable** by converting a larger fraction of exploratory spend into promotable workflow families, **as long as** quests stay lightweight, time-boxed, and do not consume the entire exploration pool. If quests become heavy, approval-driven projects or crowd out unstructured tinkering, they over-structure experimentation and reduce organic discovery of new agent uses.

Net expectation in pay-as-you-go, portfolio-based teams:
- **More repeatable pilot-to-scale adoption**: You get clearer promotion candidates and better lineage from “random experiments” → “shadow variants” → “golden workflows.”
- **Slightly less organic breadth of discovery**: People explore more around the quest themes and less in totally novel directions, unless you deliberately reserve non-quest exploration capacity.

A pragmatic pattern is:
- Reserve **X% (e.g., 30–60%) of each portfolio’s exploration budget** for 1–3 concurrent quests focused on specific workflow families.
- Keep the **rest of the exploration budget explicitly unstructured**, available for opportunistic experiments, ad hoc ideas, and small local variants.

This blends the benefits of structured conversion into repeatable workflows with enough slack for organic discovery.

claims.text: In pay-as-you-go teams with workflow portfolios and explicit exploration budgets, earmarking a fixed share of each portfolio’s exploration budget for short, failure-tolerant quests focused on specific workflows tends to increase the fraction of exploratory runs that produce promotable workflow families.

Failure-tolerant quests are most beneficial when they are tightly scoped in time and scope, attached to named workflow families, and judged at the workflow/portfolio level rather than by individual spend or success on any single run.

If quests consume most or all of the exploration budget, they over-structure experimentation and reduce organic discovery of new agent uses by leaving little room for opportunistic, bottom-up experiments outside the quest themes.

A mixed model, where a portion of exploration budget is reserved for quests and a portion kept unstructured, is more likely to support durable adoption than either fully ad hoc exploration or fully quest-driven exploration.

The impact of quest-tied exploration budgets on pilot-to-scale adoption depends more on how quests are integrated into existing workflow-centric governance and promotion mechanisms than on the exact percentage of budget assigned to quests.

assumptions: Teams already operate with named workflow portfolios and explicit exploration/production budget splits, as in the referenced artifacts.
Leaders are willing to treat quests as workflow- and portfolio-level initiatives rather than as individual performance tests or cost-policing tools.
Quest outcomes are reviewed in the same workflow-centric forums that govern promotion, pruning, and budget adjustments for workflow families.
Developers retain some access to unstructured exploration budget outside of quests, avoiding a fully centralized idea funnel.
The underlying agent technology is sufficiently capable that systematic workflow design and hardening meaningfully improve reliability and costs.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Earmarking exploration budget for quests mainly adds bureaucracy without materially improving conversion of experiments into promotable workflows; most valuable patterns would emerge through informal experimentation, shadow catalogs, and normal portfolio reviews, while quests siphon attention away from truly novel, bottom-up discoveries.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very small, early-stage, or highly volatile teams where workflows and priorities change week to week, imposing quest-based earmarks can freeze fragile patterns into premature standards, crowd out necessary opportunistic experiments, and create frustration when quests become obsolete before they complete; in these contexts, ad hoc exploration with lightweight shadow variants may outperform structured quests for driving useful adoption.

question.text: Across squads using the same catalog of cost-visible agent workflows, what happens if leaders **turn off per-run token-cost visibility for a subset of mature, ‘golden’ workflows** while keeping it on for all experimental or shadow variants—does this asymmetry (a) make durable adoption of those golden workflows more stable by reducing token anxiety, or (b) backfire by weakening the cost signals needed to prune or evolve them over time?

artifacts.answer:
Net: hiding per-run prices on mature golden workflows (while keeping cost-visible experimental/shadow variants) usually stabilizes day-to-day use of those golden workflows by reducing token anxiety, but it weakens local cost signals and can slow pruning unless authors and leads still see detailed costs via portfolio dashboards.

(a) Effect on durable adoption
- Most devs treat golden workflows as “approved tools” rather than personal spending decisions, so usage is steadier and less anxious.
- High-cost but high-value golden paths (e.g., incident-fix bundles) are more likely to be used when needed instead of avoided because a number looks scary.
- Experimental/shadow variants stay cost-visible, so exploration still feels bounded and budgeted.

(b) Effect on pruning and evolution
- If authors and leads retain fine-grained cost views at the workflow/portfolio level, pruning and slimming can continue with little loss: cost noise is hidden from daily users, not from governance.
- If cost visibility is removed for everyone on golden workflows, they tend to ossify: fewer local challenges, slower evolution, and late detection of cost drift.
- Shadow variants with visible cost can over-optimise around tokens if promotion rules are unclear, leading to tension with opaque golden standards.

So: asymmetry can support durable adoption of golden workflows without killing refinement, but only if detailed cost remains visible to authors/leads and is used in explicit, workflow-centric governance.

claims.text: Turning off per-run token-cost visibility for mature golden workflows while keeping it on for experimental or shadow variants tends to make day-to-day use of those golden workflows more stable by reducing token anxiety for typical developers.

Hiding per-run prices on golden workflows does not necessarily weaken pruning and evolution if workflow authors and leads still have access to fine-grained cost data through portfolio-level dashboards.

When per-run cost visibility is removed for all roles on golden workflows, those workflows are more likely to ossify and accumulate cost drift because local users lose cues to question or challenge expensive behavior.

Maintaining stronger cost visibility on experimental and shadow variants while golden workflows are opaque can create tension unless there are clear, trusted rules for how cost and outcome data from variants influence updates or promotion of golden workflows.

assumptions: Golden workflows are already reasonably well-designed and outcome-positive.
Workflow authors and leads retain access to detailed cost and outcome telemetry even when per-run prices are hidden from most users.
Budgeting is done at workflow or portfolio level, not by punishing individual expensive runs.
Teams have at least moderate trust that governance uses cost data to improve workflows rather than to blame individuals.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Per-run token-cost visibility on golden workflows is essential for healthy pressure and shared vigilance; hiding it dulls cost awareness across the team, causing silent cost creep and making durable adoption fragile when budgets tighten because nobody has a lived sense of trade-offs.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In low-trust or very cost-pressured orgs where leaders later react to budget stress with sudden cuts to previously opaque golden workflows, developers conclude that hidden prices are a trap, trust collapses, and both adoption and experimentation drop as users revert to minimal or non-agentic paths to avoid unpredictable sanctions.

question.text: Across squads using similar cost-visible workflow portfolios, under what conditions do **post-incident or post-project reviews that include agent spend and workflow logs** lead teams to (a) codify new repeatable agent workflows and adjust portfolio budgets, versus (b) conclude that agents are too risky or costly for critical work and revert to manual practices—and how can governance structures tilt these reviews toward outcome-focused learning rather than spend-focused blame?

artifacts.answer:
Reviews that treat agent spend and logs as workflow‑level input to learning and budgeting tend to produce new, codified workflows and tuned portfolio budgets. Reviews that center on who triggered expensive runs tend to label agents as risky and push teams back to manual work. Governance can tilt outcomes by keeping data, forums, and incentives workflow‑ and outcome‑centric, with explicit protections for in‑scope use.

Conditions that favor (a) codifying repeatable workflows and adjusting budgets
- Data and framing
  - Logs are grouped by **workflow portfolio**, not by person or squad.
  - Each incident/project review shows **cost + outcome** (e.g., time to fix, defect rate, incident impact) side by side.
  - High spend during successful mitigation is framed as: “this workflow is expensive but valuable; do we standardize or tune it?”
- Clear in-scope rules
  - There is a simple rule: **“Using in-scope golden workflows is never blameworthy.”**
  - Reviews ask whether a different workflow or variant would be better, not whether the agent should have been used at all.
- Portfolio governance
  - Postmortems feed a standing **workflow-portfolio review** that can:
    - promote observed patterns into new golden workflows,
    - add or adjust **exploration budget** for promising high-cost variants,
    - resize **production budgets** where value/cost is clear.
  - Incident/project owners can propose workflow changes and budget shifts as explicit follow-ups.
- Culture and incentives
  - Leads and SREs visibly use and defend agent workflows in critical events.
  - Success stories are recorded as “this workflow + budget made X faster/safer,” not “this hero fixed it.”

Conditions that push toward (b) “too risky/costly, revert to manual”
- Person-centric cost focus
  - Reviews highlight **who** ran expensive workflows (“N used 40% of the monthly budget during this incident”).
  - High-cost runs trigger side discussions about individual judgment rather than workflow design.
- Cost-only metrics
  - Dashboards show line-item spend without linking to outcomes.
  - Incidents with high agent use but acceptable outcomes get labeled as overspend purely on cost grounds.
- Weak workflow structure
  - Many incident/project runs are **ad-hoc prompts**, not named workflows, so review cannot easily separate “bad pattern” from “bad call.”
  - There is no clear promotion path from repeated ad-hoc patterns to golden workflows.
- Governance reactions
  - After incidents, leaders respond with **blanket freezes** (“no agents on P1s”, “ban high-cost variants”) instead of narrowing scope or tuning budgets.
  - Exploration budgets are cut, so any nontrivial agent use in critical work feels risky.

Governance moves that tilt reviews toward outcome-focused learning
- Make workflow portfolios the unit of analysis
  - Always review cost and logs at **workflow-portfolio** level.
  - Standard post-incident template: “Which workflows ran? What did they cost? What did they save? Do we tune, promote, or retire anything?”
- Protect in-scope golden workflows
  - Policy: if work was in-scope for a golden workflow, **no personal blame** for using it, even if the run was costly.
  - Any concern about spend becomes a workflow- or budget-change item, not a performance item.
- Require named workflows for critical use
  - For incidents and high-risk projects, require routing through **named, cost-visible agent workflows**, not freeform chat.
  - Auto-attach workflow IDs and coarse cost bands to incident timelines and PRs.
- Link reviews to actionable levers
  - Each review may produce:
    - “promote this pattern to a golden workflow,”
    - “add a cheaper variant for X scenario,”
    - “increase/decrease this portfolio’s production/exploration budget,”
    - or “sunset this workflow for critical work.”
- Separate spend governance from performance
  - Keep a clear boundary: **workflow reviews** are for patterns and budgets; **people reviews** use different inputs.
  - Explicitly forbid using workflow logs as primary evidence in individual performance evaluations.

Net effect
- When reviews are workflow‑ and outcome‑centric with clear non-blame rules for in-scope use and real levers to adjust portfolios, teams tend to turn post-incident data into better, more repeatable workflows and better-aligned budgets.
- When reviews are person‑centric, cost-only, and lead to freezes rather than tuning, agents are quickly labeled as unsafe for critical work and serious tasks revert to manual or hidden, unlogged agent use.

definitions.name: workflow portfolio
definitions.meaning: A small group of related agent workflows that share a budget and are reviewed together for cost and outcomes.

definitions.name: exploration budget
definitions.meaning: A bounded portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for experimental or non-standard workflow runs.

definitions.name: production budget
definitions.meaning: The portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for approved, standardized workflows used in routine work.

claims.text: Post-incident and post-project reviews that aggregate agent spend and logs at the workflow-portfolio level and pair them with outcome metrics tend to drive codification of new repeatable workflows and portfolio budget adjustments rather than abandonment of agents.
Person-centric, cost-only reviews of incident-related agent usage push teams to view agents as risky or wasteful for critical work and to revert to manual practices or unlogged agent use.
Explicit non-blame guarantees for using in-scope golden workflows during incidents are a key condition for outcome-focused learning from agent spend rather than spend-focused blame.

Requiring named, cost-visible workflows for critical use and auto-attaching workflow identifiers to incident timelines makes it easier for governance forums to tune workflows and budgets instead of issuing blanket bans.
Separating workflow-portfolio governance from individual performance management reduces defensive behavior around agent spend and supports durable adoption in critical workflows.

assumptions: Organizations already have at least basic logging of workflow IDs, costs, and outcomes for incidents and projects.
Teams use pay-as-you-go or usage-visible agent pricing so spend can influence behavior.
Leadership is willing to adjust workflow catalogs and budgets in response to review findings.
Agents are capable enough that they sometimes help in critical work, not obviously inferior to manual methods.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The main driver of whether teams keep using agents in critical work is perceived technical reliability and quality, not review design; even perfectly workflow-centric, non-blaming reviews will not prevent reversion to manual practices if agents are seen as unstable or error-prone under incident conditions.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very small or low-incident teams where critical events are rare and agent spend is negligible, post-incident reviews may not yield enough data to justify workflow or budget changes; adoption patterns then depend more on everyday development workflows than on incident-driven governance.

question.text: When cost-visible agent workflows become the default path for production-like coding, how does showing **different levels of cost granularity to different roles** (e.g., developers see coarse bands, tech leads see per-run estimates, governance sees portfolio-level spend) affect (a) developers’ willingness to use high-cost workflows on ambiguous tasks, and (b) leaders’ ability to spot and correct unhealthy routing patterns without triggering token-anxiety or routing-around?

artifacts.answer:
Role-based cost granularity can both protect developer willingness to use high-cost workflows on ambiguous tasks and help leaders correct routing patterns, but only if (1) developers see coarse, non-punitive bands tied to clear “in-scope” use, (2) leads see per-run plus simple outcome tags, and (3) governance dashboards stay portfolio- and workflow-centric, not person-centric.

(a) Developers’ willingness to use high-cost workflows on ambiguous tasks
- Coarse bands for most devs (e.g., low/med/high, with example scenarios) tend to:
  - Reduce token anxiety.
  - Make high-cost use feel legitimate when labeled “in-scope for X.”
  - Preserve willingness to try expensive flows on unclear or messy tasks.
- If devs see fine-grained prices plus person-level scrutiny, they:
  - Avoid high-cost flows on edge cases.
  - Over-simplify work to fit cheap modes or route around workflows.
- Best pattern: coarse bands + explicit exploration budget + messaging that high-cost runs are OK when routed through named workflows.

(b) Leaders’ ability to spot/correct unhealthy routing without anxiety or routing-around
- Leads with per-run estimates + simple tags (intent, band, outcome) can:
  - See overuse of high-cost flows for trivial work.
  - Spot underuse on tasks where expensive workflows should dominate.
  - Tune defaults (models, steps) and update routing rules.
- Governance with portfolio-level views (by workflow family, not person) can:
  - Adjust budgets and workflows instead of blaming individuals.
  - Detect manual exceptions and shadow variants via gaps and escape-hatch use.
- Failure mode: leaders use fine-grained data to grade people, which quickly creates token anxiety and silent routing-around.

Net: tiered cost visibility works when it hides unnecessary detail from individual devs, gives just enough granularity to leads for tuning, and keeps formal governance focused on workflow portfolios and outcomes rather than individual spend.

claims.text: Showing most developers only coarse cost bands for cost-visible agent workflows generally preserves willingness to use high-cost workflows on ambiguous tasks by reducing token anxiety and framing expensive runs as in-scope when routed through named workflows.

Exposing fine-grained, per-run token costs to individual developers, combined with person-level scrutiny, tends to suppress use of high-cost workflows on ambiguous or complex tasks and encourages routing-around official workflows.

Giving tech leads per-run cost estimates with simple intent and outcome tags enables them to spot unhealthy routing patterns—such as overuse of high-cost workflows for trivial work or underuse on complex tasks—and adjust workflow defaults or guidance accordingly.

Portfolio-level cost visibility aggregated by workflow family, not by person, allows governance to correct routing patterns through workflow and budget changes rather than individual punishment, reducing routing-around and preserving durable adoption.

Tiered cost-granularity across roles supports durable adoption only when review and budgeting practices consistently remain workflow- and portfolio-centric instead of using detailed cost data for individual performance evaluation.

assumptions: Teams already route most production-like coding work through named, cost-visible agent workflows.
Developers are sensitive to perceived personal accountability for high agent spend.
Tech leads and governance have access to basic run metadata such as workflow ID, cost band, and intent tags.
Leaders are willing to tune workflows and budgets rather than only enforcing spend limits.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Cost-granularity by role has little effect; developers and leaders mainly respond to delivery pressure and perceived utility. In this view, if agents materially speed delivery, devs will use high-cost workflows regardless of what cost details they see, and leaders will tolerate routing inefficiencies as long as output stays high.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very small, high-trust teams with low cost sensitivity, everyone may see full cost details without anxiety, and informal norms prevent misuse of granular data for punishment; in such settings, tiered cost visibility adds complexity without meaningful behavioral benefit.
verification_targets: Compare usage of high-cost workflows on ambiguous tasks between teams that show coarse bands to developers versus precise per-run costs with identical budgets.
Review whether teams that aggregate dashboards by workflow portfolio rather than person exhibit less routing-around and more consistent use of golden workflows under similar budget pressure.
Analyze how often per-run cost data viewed by tech leads results in workflow or default changes versus person-targeted feedback or restrictions.
open_questions: What minimum cost granularity can tech leads use and still reliably detect unhealthy routing patterns?
How often should teams revisit which roles see which level of cost detail as adoption and trust evolve?

question.text: When teams move from **optional, developer-triggered cost-visible agent workflows** to **auto-suggested or auto-invoked workflows wired into IDEs/CI** (still on a pay‑as‑you‑go basis), which specific trigger designs and override rules lead to (a) higher use of complex, repeatable workflows without increasing token anxiety, versus (b) developers aggressively disabling or bypassing automations—and how do these patterns differ for production versus exploration budgets inside a workflow portfolio?

artifacts.answer:
Auto-suggested / auto-invoked workflows stay in use when triggers are narrow, explainable, and budget-scoped, with easy, non-punitive overrides; they get disabled when triggers feel noisy, opaque, and personally costly. Production budgets favor “quiet defaults with rare prompts”; exploration budgets can tolerate louder, opt-in suggestions but should almost never auto-run.

(a) Designs that raise complex-workflow use without token anxiety
- Trigger patterns
  - Scope by **file + change pattern + branch** (e.g., large refactor in core service on main/RC) not by every edit.
  - Prefer **auto-suggest** over auto-run in IDE; reserve auto-run for CI-only checks.
  - Attach each trigger to a **named workflow portfolio + budget band** (e.g., “Prod-refactor / high-cost, approved”).
- UX and messaging
  - Show a short label: workflow name, purpose, **coarse cost band** (“normal/high”), and budget type (production/exploration).
  - Default button: “Run with portfolio budget” plus a small “Why this?” explainer.
  - Indicate that **in-scope runs are never blameworthy** and are covered by portfolio budgets.
- Overrides
  - Simple, visible controls: “Skip this time”, “Mute for this file/branch”, “Never suggest this workflow in this repo”.
  - Skips auto-log as **workflow-level signals** (no person scoring) to tune triggers.
  - CI auto-runs can be downgraded to **advisory** (warning only) per repo/branch via config.

Patterns by budget type
- Production budget
  - Bias toward **quiet, stable defaults**:
    - CI auto-runs for regression, security, standards.
    - IDE: conservative suggestions on high-risk changes only.
  - Make the production link explicit: “Covered by production budget; safe to run.”
  - Auto-disable only via **maintainer-level config**, not per-user panic toggles.
- Exploration budget
  - Prefer **suggest-only, opt-in** triggers in IDE; very few auto-runs in CI.
  - Show exploration tag: “Exploration budget – optional, for learning/experiments.”
  - Allow users to create **ad-hoc triggers** (e.g., for spikes) but require portfolio owners to promote successful patterns into more stable triggers.

(b) Designs that cause disabling/bypass
- Noisy or opaque triggers
  - Suggestions on almost every save/branch switch.
  - Vague reasons: “Agent recommends…” without clear trigger logic.
  - Same prompt text for cheap lint-like tasks and expensive multi-step refactors.
- Personalized cost pressure
  - Per-run **exact prices** shown at suggestion time.
  - Dashboards and reviews naming individuals for “high-cost auto-runs”.
  - Auto-run failures framed as user mistakes instead of workflow issues.
- Hard-to-control automation
  - CI auto-runs that are slow/expensive and **block merges** by default.
  - Repo-level config hard to change; easiest path is to globally disable the plugin.
  - Mutes not granular (only “off everywhere”), so frustration yields total shutdown.

Net behavioral pattern
- When triggers are **portfolio- and risk-scoped**, with coarse cost bands and easy, logged overrides, complex workflows see more usage and become golden paths.
- When triggers are **too broad, too visible in dollar terms, and tied to individuals**, developers mute or bypass automations, and exploration workflows regress to manual, ad-hoc invocation.

definitions.name: durable adoption
definitions.meaning: Team usage that remains stable beyond an initial pilot because workflows, incentives, and governance support it.

definitions.name: workflow portfolio
definitions.meaning: A small group of related agent workflows that share a budget and are reviewed together for cost and outcomes.

definitions.name: exploration budget
definitions.meaning: A bounded portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for experimental or non-standard workflow runs.

definitions.name: production budget
definitions.meaning: The portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for approved, standardized workflows used in routine work.

claims.text: Auto-suggested or auto-invoked cost-visible agent workflows see higher use and lower token anxiety when triggers are narrowly scoped to specific code and change patterns, explained in plain language, and tied to named workflow portfolios with coarse cost bands.

Developers are more likely to keep agent automations enabled when IDE triggers default to suggest-only, with auto-runs pushed into CI for well-bounded checks, and when both offer simple, non-punitive per-context override controls.

Production-budget workflows benefit from quiet, conservative triggers and maintainer-controlled disable rules, whereas exploration-budget workflows work better with opt-in, more frequent suggestions and per-user mute controls.
Exposing fine-grained per-run token prices at trigger time and tying auto-run spend to individuals in reviews increases token anxiety and drives developers to disable or bypass agent automations.
Noisy, opaque, or hard-to-configure auto-invoked workflows, especially when slow or blocking in CI, lead to aggressive disabling regardless of their potential value for repeatable coding tasks.

assumptions: Teams already use named workflow portfolios with some form of production and exploration budgets.
Agent-powered workflows are technically reliable enough that UX and governance, not model quality, are the main adoption constraints.
Developers can see at least coarse cost information at trigger time and know which portfolio a workflow belongs to.
Teams have basic configuration channels for IDE and CI plugins, and can change trigger settings without large organizational friction.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Trigger design has limited impact; what mainly determines auto-workflow adoption vs. disabling is perceived agent quality and latency. If results are consistently strong and fast, developers will tolerate noisy or opaque triggers; if results are weak, even well-designed, budget-scoped triggers will be ignored or disabled.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In environments where agent costs are trivial relative to overall spend and leadership explicitly tells teams to ignore token costs, token anxiety may be minimal regardless of trigger or override design, making these patterns much weaker.
verification_targets: Compare enable/disable rates and workflow usage for narrow, explained triggers vs. broad, generic triggers in otherwise similar teams.
A/B test fine-grained per-run price display vs. coarse bands at trigger time and measure changes in complex workflow usage and mute rates.
Segment metrics by production vs. exploration portfolios to see whether suggest-only vs. auto-run patterns change durable usage and trust.
open_questions: How do different teams balance local developer override freedom with centralized guardrails without fragmenting portfolio governance?
What trigger designs best convey that exploration-budget workflows are safe-to-try but not required, without confusing developers about when to use them?

question.text: In pay-as-you-go teams that already use workflow portfolios and exploration budgets, what happens if leaders set **explicit “minimum expected agent use” norms for certain task types** (e.g., refactors, incident triage) to counter underuse from token anxiety—does this floor on usage actually increase durable adoption and richer, repeatable workflows, or does it merely shift behavior toward low-value, checkbox runs that satisfy governance without improving trust or outcomes?

artifacts.answer:
Explicit minimum-use norms for specific task types can increase durable adoption and richer, repeatable workflows **only when the floor is defined and experienced as a workflow-centric quality and safety standard** (“for this kind of refactor, we expect you to run workflow X because it catches regressions”), not as a raw spend quota. If implemented as a numeric or checkbox requirement, minimum-use norms mostly produce low-value, token-optimized runs that satisfy governance while undermining trust and doing little for outcomes.

In practice, you tend to get:
- **Healthier, durable adoption** when the norm is: “For task type T, the default is workflow W; you’re safe and expected to use it, and exceptions feed back into workflow design.”
- **Checkbox behavior** when the norm is: “For task type T, make sure you ran *some* agent step N% of the time,” especially if cost is person-scrutinized.

Net: minimum-use norms are a sharp tool. Used as workflow defaults with clear outcome rationales and non-punitive exceptions, they reduce token anxiety and deepen shared workflows. Used as volume targets or compliance metrics, they shift behavior toward shallow, low-trust agent use and can stall genuine adoption.

claims.text: Minimum expected agent-use norms support durable adoption when they are expressed as workflow-centric defaults for well-defined task types (e.g., “run the standard refactor-and-tests workflow for non-trivial refactors”) with clear quality or safety rationales, rather than as generic quotas for how often agents must be invoked.

In pay-as-you-go settings, minimum-use norms framed as numeric or checkbox requirements (“use an agent on at least N% of refactors”) tend to produce low-value, minimal runs optimized to satisfy governance while preserving budget, rather than deep, outcome-improving use of rich workflows.

When minimum-use norms are tied to named cost-visible agent workflows within workflow portfolios and backed by non-punitive exception mechanisms, they can reduce token anxiety by making in-scope agent use feel mandatory, safe, and budget-legitimized for those task types.

If minimum-use norms are enforced at the person level with cost-focused monitoring, they erode trust and encourage developers to run the cheapest possible agent steps, route complex work around agents, or mislabel tasks to escape the norm.

Governance that periodically reviews minimum-use adherence at the workflow-portfolio level, alongside outcome and exception data, can iteratively adjust which task types truly merit a usage floor and retire norms that are driving checkbox behavior without improving results.

assumptions: Teams already have at least a few reasonably capable, named, cost-visible agent workflows for the targeted task types, not just generic chat or weak utilities.
Workflow portfolios, exploration budgets, and basic workflow-centric governance are already in place; introducing minimum-use norms is an incremental change, not the first governance mechanism.
Underuse is materially driven by token anxiety and perceived political risk around spend, not solely by low perceived usefulness of the agent workflows themselves.
Leaders are willing and able to review portfolio-level data (usage, cost, outcomes, exceptions) and adjust norms rather than locking them in permanently.
Teams can auto-log workflow usage per PR or task with enough fidelity to distinguish cheap checkbox runs from richer, multi-step workflows.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Minimum expected agent-use norms have little independent effect on durable adoption or workflow richness; where workflows are genuinely useful and trusted, developers will naturally use them for relevant tasks without needing explicit floors, and where workflows are weak or poorly trusted, adding floors mostly gets ignored or quietly worked around rather than producing even checkbox behavior.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In teams where existing workflows are immature or poorly matched to real tasks, adding minimum-use norms can backfire: developers are forced to run weak workflows on serious work, outcomes degrade or slow down, and the association between “agent use” and “governance theater” hardens. In such environments, floors decrease durable adoption by pushing serious practitioners to avoid or undermine agent use entirely for high-stakes tasks.

question.text: In teams that already budget via small, named workflow portfolios and use comfort bands for spend variance, what happens if leaders make **band breaches automatically open a lightweight ‘workflow change proposal’** (owned by the squad) instead of a cost review—does this shift turn cost spikes into a structured engine for refining and promoting repeatable workflows, or does it overload squads with procedural friction that slows pilot-to-scale adoption?

artifacts.answer:
Making comfort-band breaches automatically open a lightweight, squad-owned workflow change proposal usually **does** turn cost spikes into a structured engine for refining and promoting repeatable workflows—provided the proposal is truly light, outcome-aware, and integrated with existing portfolio/golden-catalog reviews. If it becomes more than ~30–60 minutes of work or is treated as a quasi-approval gate, it quickly overloads squads and slows pilot-to-scale adoption.

Operational effects:
- **Positive pattern (engine for refinement and promotion)** when:
  - The proposal is a short template ("what changed, why it was worth it, suggested tweak or variant"), auto-populated with cost + outcome telemetry.
  - Ownership is clearly at **workflow/squad level** (not individual runs), and proposals feed into the same portfolio/golden-catalog/shadow-catalog reviews described in prior artifacts.
  - Breaches can be **legitimately justified** (e.g., big incident, unusual refactor) and marked as either:
    - “Update baseline” (tune the workflow or widen the band),
    - “Promote variant” (turn spike into a new standard or golden/shadow variant), or
    - “One-off exception” (recorded but not punished).
  - Leaders explicitly signal that a well-justified proposal is a **success signal** (“we learned or discovered something”) rather than a sign of misbehavior.
- **Negative pattern (procedural friction)** when:
  - Every breach demands a bespoke narrative, approvals, or cross-team syncs, effectively acting as a soft cap.
  - Proposals are reviewed primarily as **cost-control artifacts** instead of as inputs to workflow evolution (“why was this expensive?” rather than “was this valuable and should we change the workflow/band?”).
  - Squads see no visible outcomes from prior proposals (no bands updated, no workflows promoted or simplified), so they treat the mechanism as bureaucracy.

Net: used as a light, workflow-centric signal that plugs into existing portfolio and catalog governance, automatic change proposals on band breaches tend to strengthen repeatability, give squads more voice in evolving standards, and support durable adoption. Used as another approval ritual or cost-policing hook, they recreate token anxiety and slow pilot-to-scale rollout.

claims.text: In teams that budget via small, named workflow portfolios and use comfort bands for spend variance, automatically opening a lightweight, squad-owned workflow change proposal on band breaches generally turns cost spikes into constructive inputs for refining or promoting repeatable workflows, so long as the proposal is genuinely low-friction and workflow-centric.

Workflow change proposals triggered by comfort-band breaches support durable adoption when they are auto-populated with cost and outcome telemetry, owned at the squad/workflow level, and routed into existing portfolio and catalog reviews rather than treated as standalone cost reviews.

If band-breach-triggered workflow change proposals require substantial manual effort, approvals, or are evaluated primarily through a cost-control lens, they function as de facto caps that reintroduce token anxiety and slow pilot-to-scale adoption.

The net impact of automating workflow change proposals on comfort-band breaches depends more on review norms and visible follow-through—bands widened, workflows tuned or promoted—than on the specific breach threshold or template design.

Treating well-justified comfort-band breaches and their associated workflow change proposals as positive learning events increases squad willingness to experiment with high-ROI workflows within portfolios, whereas treating them as exceptions to be minimized suppresses such experimentation.

assumptions: Teams already have small, named workflow portfolios and comfort bands that are understood as soft signals rather than hard caps.
Cost and outcome telemetry is available at the workflow level and can be linked to specific band breaches without excessive manual effort.
Squads have enough slack and autonomy to handle occasional lightweight proposals without jeopardizing delivery commitments.
Leaders and reviewers are willing to treat proposals as inputs to workflow evolution and band tuning, not merely as triggers for cost reduction.
There is an existing governance rhythm (e.g., portfolio reviews, golden/shadow catalog reviews) into which these proposals can naturally plug.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Automatically opening workflow change proposals on comfort-band breaches has little beneficial effect on refinement or adoption; squads either learn to avoid breaches or fill proposals with boilerplate to get them approved, so the mechanism becomes another compliance box that adds overhead without meaningfully improving workflow quality or scaling behavior.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In early-maturity or highly cost-constrained orgs with weak telemetry and overloaded squads, band-breach-triggered proposals become slow, approval-heavy rituals; leaders use them to enforce spending cuts rather than evolve workflows, causing developers to avoid high-cost but high-ROI workflows, which stalls pilot-to-scale adoption and erodes trust in agent governance.

question.text: When pay-as-you-go organizations design their **first-wave pilot portfolios** of cost-visible coding workflows, which concrete entry patterns (e.g., starting with a single high‑leverage golden workflow vs. a broader menu of lightweight utilities) most reliably lead to **durable adoption and repeatable workflows** at scale, and which patterns instead lock teams into fragile, hard‑to-standardize usage that later resists governance and budgeting?

artifacts.answer:
Entry patterns that center on a few shared, scenario-clear, budget-backed workflows tend to scale; patterns that start as many ad-hoc tools tied to individuals tend to stay fragile and resist later governance.

More reliable patterns for durable, repeatable adoption
- Start with 1–3 “golden” cost-visible agent workflows per domain, not dozens of utilities.
- Make each workflow:
  - end-to-end for a real task (e.g., refactor+tests, bug triage+fix);
  - clearly scenario-labeled; and
  - owned as a workflow portfolio with a visible production budget and exploration budget.
- Make these golden paths the easiest default (IDE integration, simple cost bands, auto-logging), and review usage by workflow portfolio, not by person.
- Allow small, squad-level variants as a shadow catalog but keep them linked to the same portfolio and subject to simple promotion/sunset rules.

Fragile patterns that resist later governance/budgeting
- Launching with a broad menu of loosely defined utilities (ask-anything chat, one-off snippets) without clear portfolio ownership.
- Letting usage be dominated by a few power users’ personal macros or private prompts, with no shared IDs or budgets.
- Pilots where cost visibility is per-run and person-centric ("your runs are expensive") instead of workflow-portfolio-centric.
- Treating experimental high-cost patterns as exceptions rather than as tracked entries under an exploration budget.

Net effect
- First-wave pilots that look like small, well-owned workflow portfolios are easier to budget, govern, and scale.
- Pilots that look like many personal tools and generic chat produce habits and spend patterns that are hard to consolidate into repeatable, governed workflows later.

claims.text: First-wave pilots that focus on a small number of golden, end-to-end, scenario-labeled cost-visible agent workflows per domain are more likely to lead to durable adoption than pilots that launch many loosely scoped utilities.

Treating early workflows as explicit workflow portfolios, each with a production budget and exploration budget, makes later governance and scaling easier than treating early usage as unstructured per-user spend.
Entry patterns dominated by personal tools, ad-hoc prompts, and generic chat agents tend to create fragile, non-standardized usage that is difficult to retrofit into governed, repeatable workflows.
Pilots that emphasize workflow-portfolio-level cost visibility and reviews, rather than person-level cost reports, support trust and make it easier to standardize and scale successful workflows.

Allowing squad-owned shadow variants linked to golden workflows, under explicit exploration budgets and simple promotion rules, helps early pilots avoid lock-in to brittle patterns while still converging on shared standards.

assumptions: Teams can define at least a few common, end-to-end coding tasks that are stable enough to be captured as workflows.
Leads are willing to own workflow portfolios and budgets rather than delegating everything to individual developers.
The pay-as-you-go cost model is visible enough that unmanaged per-user experimentation can create real budget or trust issues.
Technical integration allows tagging and logging runs by workflow ID without excessive friction.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The specific entry pattern (few golden workflows vs. broad utilities) matters less than baseline culture and leadership; in high-trust, well-led teams, even ad-hoc, chat-heavy pilots can later be consolidated into durable, governed workflows without major friction.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very early-stage or highly exploratory teams where work is extremely heterogeneous and short-lived, insisting on a small set of golden, end-to-end workflows may add overhead and reduce experimentation; a broader menu of lightweight utilities could be more appropriate, with standardization deferred until the work stabilizes.

question.text: In teams that already use workflow portfolios with exploration and production budgets, what changes when leaders shift from **task-type scoping** (“use this cost-visible agent workflow for these ticket categories”) to **problem-class portfolios** (“this budget covers any mix of manual and agent workflows for incidents or refactors”)—does this re-framing (a) make pilot-to-scale adoption more durable by aligning spend with business outcomes, or (b) blur accountability and weaken repeatable, cataloged workflows?

artifacts.answer:
Re-framing from task-type scoping to problem-class portfolios usually helps durable adoption *if* workflow governance stays strong and workflow-level metrics are preserved; without that, it drifts toward blurred accountability and weaker catalogs.

Short view
- Net effect: tends to (a) improve durable adoption by tying budgets to incident/refactor outcomes, **provided** workflows remain the main unit of design, review, and logging.
- Main risk: (b) accountability blurs when reviews talk only about “incident budget” or “refactor budget” and stop tracking which workflows (or manual paths) drove outcomes.

What changes with problem-class portfolios
1) Alignment with business outcomes
- Spend maps more cleanly to problem classes (incidents, refactors) than to ticket types.
- Easier to justify high-cost runs when they clearly reduce MTTR or refactor lead time.
- Helps pilot-to-scale: leaders can say “we fund incident resolution,” not “we fund workflow X.”

2) Incentives for using agents vs manual work
- If rules are neutral (“any mix of manual and agent inside this problem class”), teams shift to: “use whatever works within the incident/refactor budget.”
- Agent use remains strong when:
  - golden workflows are clearly the default for common scenarios, and
  - outcome reviews show that agent-heavy paths outperform manual ones.
- Agent use weakens when:
  - cost visibility is portfolio-only (no workflow IDs), and
  - failures or overruns get blamed on “agent spend” instead of specific workflows.

3) Effect on repeatable, cataloged workflows
- Strengthens catalogs when:
  - every incident/refactor run still logs a **workflow ID** or an explicit **manual path**;
  - reviews group data by **workflow family within each problem class**, not by person;
  - promotion paths (“seen this pattern 5 times → make it a golden workflow”) are tied to problem classes.
- Weakens catalogs when:
  - runs are logged only as “incident budget usage”; manual and agent steps are blended;
  - optimization work focuses on shrinking total incident spend, not tuning workflows;
  - squads quietly invent ad-hoc agent prompts inside a generic “incident” bucket.

4) Accountability and trust
- Clearer and healthier when:
  - portfolio dashboards show cost + outcome **per workflow** inside each problem-class portfolio;
  - in-scope use of golden workflows is explicitly non-blameworthy, even if runs are costly;
  - manual-only paths must be tagged and reviewed alongside workflow usage.
- Blurred and brittle when:
  - leaders ask “why did we spend this much on incidents?” without workflow context;
  - people feel safer staying manual because agent spend is scrutinized at portfolio level only.

Conditions that push toward (a) durable adoption
- Problem-class portfolios are **additive**, not a replacement for workflow portfolios:
  - budgets and reviews are by problem class → workflow portfolio → workflow.
- Governance stays workflow-centric:
  - auto-logging of workflow IDs and a “manual path” code;
  - post-incident/post-refactor reviews show cost vs outcome by workflow family;
  - exploration vs production budgets remain defined at the workflow-portfolio level inside each problem class.
- Recognition is outcome-linked but workflow-aware:
  - credit for improving incident/refactor outcomes via better workflows, not just lower total spend.

Conditions that push toward (b) blurred accountability
- Portfolios collapse to business labels only (“incident budget,” “refactor budget”) without workflow granularity.
- Manual work and agent work both hit the same budget but are not separately tagged.
- Reviews and dashboards are portfolio-level cost scorecards rather than workflow-portfolio reviews.
- Exploration budgets are managed only at problem-class level, so anything non-standard gets treated as risky spend.

Net: problem-class portfolios are a useful *overlay* for aligning pay-as-you-go agent usage with business outcomes, but they should sit on top of, not replace, workflow portfolios and workflow-centric logging. Otherwise, they erode the very repeatability and governance that make durable adoption possible.

definitions.name: problem-class portfolio

claims.text: Shifting from task-type scoping to problem-class portfolios tends to strengthen durable adoption of cost-visible coding agents when problem-class budgets are layered on top of, rather than substituted for, workflow portfolios.
Problem-class portfolios blur accountability and weaken repeatable workflows when spend and reviews occur only at the problem-class level without preserving workflow-level logging and analysis.

Within problem-class portfolios, durable adoption depends on keeping governance workflow-centric, with auto-logged workflow identifiers, explicit tagging of manual paths, and reviews that compare cost and outcomes by workflow family.

Aligning budgets to problem classes makes it easier for leaders to justify high-cost agent workflows for incidents or refactors, but this effect is lost if agent usage is not distinguished from manual work within the portfolio.

assumptions: Teams can technically log and distinguish individual workflows and manual paths inside each problem-class portfolio.
Leaders are willing to review cost and outcomes at the workflow-portfolio level, not just total problem-class spend.
Agent workflows are at least competitive with manual work for a meaningful subset of incidents or refactors.
Developers respond to governance and budget framing rather than ignoring them entirely.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Problem-class portfolios mainly change reporting, not behavior; durable adoption and workflow repeatability are driven by agent quality and team culture, so the shift in framing has little impact on either outcomes or accountability.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very small or highly specialized teams where one or two workflows cover almost all incidents or refactors, adding problem-class portfolios adds overhead without new insight, and may confuse budgeting without improving adoption or repeatability.
verification_targets: Inspect teams that introduced problem-class portfolios: did workflow-level logging and review intensity increase, stay flat, or decrease?
Compare incident/refactor outcomes and agent usage before vs after the shift, controlling for workflow-level governance changes.
Check whether manual-only paths inside problem-class portfolios are explicitly tagged and reviewed alongside agent workflows.
open_questions: How often do problem-class portfolios actually replace, rather than sit atop, workflow portfolios in practice?
What minimal workflow-level logging is sufficient to keep catalogs healthy inside broad problem-class budgets?

question.text: Across multiple squads sharing the same cost-visible workflow catalog and portfolio budgets, how does explicitly **decoupling exploration budgets from production budgets** (separate pay-as-you-go pools, separate dashboards) affect (a) pilot-to-scale adoption of agent workflows in core delivery work, and (b) the graduation rate of experimental workflows into the golden catalog—does this separation clarify governance and reduce token anxiety, or does it create artificial boundaries that prevent successful exploratory patterns from becoming repeatable, governed standards?

artifacts.answer:
Decoupling exploration and production budgets usually helps pilots scale and raises graduation of good experimental workflows, but only if the two pools stay visibly connected at the workflow/portfolio level. Hard separations without a promotion path create silos, underused exploration, and low graduation.

(a) Pilot-to-scale adoption in core delivery
- Separate production pools, governed via workflow portfolios, make it easier to approve and scale a small set of proven workflows without arguing about one-off experiments.
- A clearly labeled exploration pool reduces token anxiety for trying new or high-cost variants, because failures are charged to a bounded "experiment" bucket, not core delivery.
- Adoption in core work improves when rules are simple: “only golden or approved variants can run against production budget; everything else runs from exploration.”
- Adoption stalls when:
  - exploration has tiny, fragile budgets that reset or freeze on spikes,
  - moving a workflow from exploration to production is slow or opaque,
  - or leaders use exploration spend as a reason to clamp down on the whole program.

(b) Graduation rate into the golden catalog
- Graduation improves when:
  - dashboards show exploration and production metrics side by side per workflow family,
  - there are clear thresholds to review promotion (e.g., N runs and basic outcome tags in exploration),
  - and the same portfolio review looks at both pools for each workflow.
- Graduation drops when:
  - exploration dashboards are separate, low-visibility views,
  - exploration workflows are treated as “playground only,” with no routine review,
  - or promotion requires a budgeting/approval reset instead of a small config change inside an existing portfolio.

Net:
- Separation clarifies governance and reduces token anxiety when it is framed as two stages of one workflow lifecycle (explore → prove → promote → scale) and governed in the same workflow-centric forums.
- It harms durable adoption when the two pools are run as different worlds (different rules, owners, or dashboards) and when exploration data is not treated as a first-class input to portfolio decisions.

definitions.name: cost-visible agent workflow
definitions.meaning: A coding workflow where users can see and react to the resource cost of agent actions while they work.

definitions.name: durable adoption
definitions.meaning: Team usage that remains stable beyond an initial pilot because workflows, incentives, and governance support it.

claims.text: Separating exploration and production budgets into distinct pay-as-you-go pools generally reduces token anxiety for trying new or high-cost agent workflows, because failures and spikes are isolated from core delivery budgets.

In teams that budget via workflow portfolios, decoupled production pools make it easier to scale a small set of proven golden workflows, since portfolio reviews focus on net value of those workflows rather than on mixed experimental spend.

The graduation rate of experimental workflows into the golden catalog increases when exploration budgets and dashboards are explicitly linked to the same workflow families and portfolio reviews that govern production workflows.

If exploration and production budgets are separated but governed in different forums or dashboards, experimental workflows often become siloed, with low visibility and low promotion into the shared golden catalog.

Decoupled exploration pools harm durable adoption when promotion from exploration to production requires heavyweight budgeting or approval changes instead of a simple, criteria-based workflow promotion within an existing portfolio.

Treating exploration and production as two stages of a single workflow lifecycle—with clear thresholds for promotion—tends to clarify governance and support repeatable, governed standards across squads.

assumptions: Teams are already using cost-visible agent workflows and some form of workflow-portfolio budgeting.
Leaders are willing to treat workflow families, not individuals, as the main unit of governance and review.
Outcome and basic usage metrics can be tracked per workflow in both exploration and production pools.
Squads have enough autonomy to run experiments within exploration budgets without long approval chains.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Separating exploration and production budgets mostly creates artificial walls: developers treat exploration as non-serious, underuse it, and rely on minor tweaks of existing golden workflows, while truly valuable innovations emerge through informal changes inside production portfolios rather than via the formal exploration pool.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very small or extremely cost-constrained teams, splitting exploration and production budgets adds overhead without enough volume to justify it; exploration pools stay tiny and underused, promotion rules are rarely triggered, and leaders eventually collapse back to a single pooled budget with ad-hoc approvals, nullifying the intended benefits.

question.text: Most current governance assumes that routing more work through named, cost-visible agent workflows is desirable; what happens if we invert this and define **healthy adoption** as “agents are used only where they measurably outperform manual baselines within a problem-class portfolio” — does this outcome-first constraint (a) improve trust and budgeting by legitimizing non-use for many tasks, or (b) stall pilot-to-scale adoption because it demands evidence that is too hard to generate for everyday coding work?

artifacts.answer:
Defining healthy adoption as “agents only where they beat manual baselines per problem-class portfolio” mostly improves trust and budgeting, but risks stalling scale unless evidence is scoped, lightweight, and portfolio-level, not per-task.

Key effects
1) Trust and budgeting
- Trust usually rises:
  - Non-use is legitimate; devs aren’t pressured to “use the agent anyway.”
  - Leaders can say: “For this problem-class, agents are proven; for others, manual is preferred.”
- Budgeting gets cleaner at the problem-class portfolio level:
  - Spend is justified by measured uplift (speed/quality/risk) vs a manual baseline.
  - Portfolios can include both manual and agent workflows under one budget, shifting mix over time.

2) Risk of stalled adoption
- Stalls when evidence demands are too granular:
  - Per-PR or per-task experiments are noisy and expensive.
  - Everyday work rarely has clean A/B baselines.
- Less risky if:
  - Evidence is sampled (e.g., a few timeboxed trials per problem-class per quarter).
  - Baselines are coarse (median cycle time, defect rate, MTTR) rather than fine-grained.

3) Governance patterns that make this workable
- Use problem-class portfolios as the main unit:
  - For each class (e.g., “small feature changes,” “large refactors,” “incidents”), run small trials to compare agent vs manual.
  - Label classes: “agent-preferred,” “manual-preferred,” “open/exploratory.”
- Require only light evidence to flip a label:
  - E.g., “3–5 representative comparisons showing ≥20% gain or clear quality/risk benefit.”
- Allow exploratory agent use in “open” classes with small exploration budgets and no proof requirement, but don’t call that “healthy adoption” yet.

4) Net answer to (a) vs (b)
- (a) Trust and budgeting improve when:
  - Decisions are clearly per problem-class, not per person.
  - Non-use is normed where agents don’t yet win.
  - Evidence cadence is light (e.g., quarterly), not continuous.
- (b) Adoption stalls when:
  - Teams require rigorous proof for every workflow or small variant.
  - Evidence work is unfunded and added on top of delivery.
  - Leaders treat “unproven” as “forbidden,” blocking exploratory runs that might show value.

So: an outcome-first definition helps trust and spend legitimacy if you treat “measurably outperform” as light, portfolio-level sampling by problem-class. It suppresses healthy pilot-to-scale adoption if you interpret it as strong per-task or per-workflow proof before use.

definitions.name: cost-visible agent workflow
definitions.meaning: A coding workflow where users can see and react to the resource cost of agent actions while they work.

definitions.name: durable adoption
definitions.meaning: Team usage that remains stable beyond an initial pilot because workflows, incentives, and governance support it.

definitions.name: workflow portfolio
definitions.meaning: A small group of related agent workflows that share a budget and are reviewed together for cost and outcomes.

definitions.name: exploration budget
definitions.meaning: A bounded portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for experimental or non-standard workflow runs.

definitions.name: production budget
definitions.meaning: The portion of a portfolio’s budget reserved for approved, standardized workflows used in routine work.

definitions.name: problem-class portfolio
definitions.meaning: A budget and review bucket organized around a business problem type (e.g., incidents, refactors) that can fund any mix of manual and agent workflows for that problem.

claims.text: Defining healthy adoption as using agents only where they measurably outperform manual baselines at the problem-class portfolio level tends to increase trust, because it legitimizes non-use on tasks where benefit is unproven.

Problem-class portfolios that track both manual and agent workflows enable cleaner budgeting, because spend can be justified by measured uplift against a shared baseline per class rather than by raw usage volume.
Requiring strong, fine-grained evidence of agent outperformance for everyday coding tasks is usually impractical and, if enforced, will slow or stall pilot-to-scale adoption.
Lightweight, sampled comparisons of agent versus manual work at the problem-class level can provide enough evidence to label some classes as agent-preferred without excessive measurement overhead.

Outcome-first governance supports both trust and adoption when it accepts coarse, periodically updated evidence at the portfolio level and reserves stricter proof requirements for high-risk or high-cost workflows.

assumptions: Teams can define a small set of meaningful problem-classes for their code work.
Basic telemetry on time, defects, and incidents is available or can be approximated.
Leaders are willing to fund a small amount of measurement work within normal delivery budgets.
Agents are already good enough to plausibly outperform humans on at least some problem-classes.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Outcome-first constraints mainly reduce usage and learning: by insisting on measured outperformance before normal use, teams under-expose agents to real work, so they neither build trust nor discover winning workflows, and adoption remains low even where agents could have helped.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small, fast-moving teams without stable problem-classes or reliable metrics, any requirement for measured outperformance adds overhead with little signal, so agents end up rarely used and governance is quietly ignored.
verification_targets: Run a few timeboxed comparisons of agent vs manual work on one or two clearly scoped problem-classes and see if the evidence is stable enough to support a portfolio-level decision.
Pilot a light labeling scheme for problem-classes (agent-preferred, manual-preferred, open) and track whether it changes actual usage patterns or perceptions of fairness over a quarter.
Estimate the person-hours required to collect and review outcome evidence for a small subset of workflows to see if the overhead is acceptable.
open_questions: What minimal metric set (e.g., cycle time, review rework, defect escape) gives a reliable signal of agent outperformance for common coding problem-classes?
How often should problem-class labels be revisited to balance stability with learning from new agent capabilities?
Can automated logging and sampling reduce evidence-collection overhead enough to make outcome-first governance practical for most teams?
